name: Payday Plan — allocate, lock, tick, unlock
summary: The full payday-plan journey: open the canvas, set an allocation, lock it, tick items to fire real transactions, then unlock. The healthy allocate→lock→tick path WORKS (the working contrast to Savings). The breaks are feedback (lock/unlock are invisible) and a leftover third lock-store the canonical writers never sync.

steps.title: Open Payday Plan canvas
steps.does: You tap the Payday Plan tile on the dashboard and the planning canvas slides in.
steps.wired: openPaydayPlan() — lazy-inits S.activePlan, runs rolloverIfNeeded, renders root
steps.plain: Opening the collection in Postman — the canvas is the workspace you fire everything from. First open it creates an empty plan (lockedAt:null) and saves it.

steps.title: Open a category sub-screen (Savings)
steps.does: You tap the Savings row on the canvas and its sub-screen slides over.
steps.plain: Drilling into one folder inside the collection — the Savings sub-screen lists each goal/bucket with its planned amount.

steps.title: Set an allocation override
steps.does: You pick an amount for a bucket and Save. The modal writes the override.
steps.wired: openEditPaydaySavings save() → BRAIN.plan.setOverride('savings', bucket, amt, {}, PLAN_OVERRIDE_SET)
steps.plain: Editing the request body before you send it — you're saying 'this cycle, put $X into this goal'. It's stored as an override, not yet executed.

steps.title: Over-allocation gate (INV-32)
steps.does: If the new savings amount would push total savings past the available surplus, the write is refused and the modal stays open with the reason.
steps.wired: BRAIN.plan.setOverride savings branch — simulates wouldBeSavingsTotal vs snap.derived.surplus
steps.plain: A validation rule on the endpoint: it rejects a request that would over-spend the pool (returns ok:false reason:inv32-over-allocation). Reductions always pass. The healthy guard that Savings-the-surface lacks.

steps.title: Lock the plan
steps.does: You tap Lock plan, confirm, and the plan commits — lockedAt is stamped and mirrored to the allocation store.
steps.plain: Sending the request to commit the plan. The endpoint is correct (it stamps the lock + mirrors it), BUT the screen gives no visible proof — the canvas frame is pixel-identical before and after the tap. From John's seat: the POST returned 200 but the UI never updated, so you can't tell it worked.

steps.title: Lock state lives in 3 stores — only 2 sync
steps.does: lockedAt is written to S.activePlan AND mirrored to BRAIN.allocation's localStorage. The legacy PLAN-mode tile reads a THIRD store — the slyght_payday_plan.locked boolean — which the canonical writers never set.
steps.wired: lock writes lockedAt to (1) S.activePlan.lockedAt + (2) slyght_payday_plan.lockedAt; legacy renderPlanMode reads (3) stored.locked
steps.plain: The same lock flag duplicated across three shadow tables. The canonical writer keeps tables (1) and (2) in step, but table (3) — the old `.locked` boolean that the legacy PLAN-mode tile still reads — is never updated. So after a canvas lock, the canvas says LOCKED and the old PLAN tile says unlocked. Drift between shadow tables that should always agree.

steps.title: Tick an item (execute the allocation)
steps.does: Post-lock you tap Mark as allocated on a bucket. paydayTick gates on locked, then fires the real transaction that credits the bucket and debits cash.
steps.file: handler index.html:13055, writer index.html:21841, tick credit index.html:21944
steps.plain: THE WORKING PATH — the contrast to Savings. Because lock committed a destination (the bucket) at step 3-4, tick has somewhere to send the money: it credits the bucket AND debits cash in one atomic envelope. recordWithAllocation fires bucket_saved_change here, exactly the rung Savings-the-surface never reaches.

steps.title: Tick is refused pre-lock
steps.does: If you try to tick before locking, paydayTick refuses and shows the lock-first hint.
steps.plain: A precondition check on the endpoint: you can't execute the plan until you've committed it. Tapping a tick pre-lock just nudges you to lock first — no money moves.

steps.title: Unlock the plan (canonical paths)
steps.does: You tap Re-plan / Unlock and the lock clears so overrides are editable again. Ticked items stay ticked.
steps.file: canvas index.html:12903, inline index.html:3588, writer index.html:22149
steps.plain: Sending the un-commit request. The endpoint clears stores (1) and (2) correctly — but like step 5 the canvas frame doesn't visibly change, so you can't confirm it landed. And it still doesn't touch store (3), the legacy `.locked` boolean.

steps.title: INV-29 — overrides frozen while locked
steps.does: While locked, setOverride/clearOverride refuse — you can't edit allocations until you unlock. Transactions, ticks and bonus edits stay editable.
steps.plain: The commit is locked: while a plan is locked the allocation-edit endpoint returns 'plan-locked' and refuses. This is why the lock has to be clean — and why the stale third store (step 6) matters: the wrong surface could disagree about whether editing is even allowed.
